chapter:
&adminguide;
Overview
The cachedb_nats module implements the &osips; CacheDB engine interface on top of NATS JetStream Key-Value stores. It provides persistent, replicated, cluster-aware key-value storage accessible from &osips; routing scripts via the standard cache_store/cache_fetch/cache_remove operations, together with several NATS-native extensions.
The module connects to a NATS JetStream-enabled server or cluster, creates a named KV bucket on first use, and maps all CacheDB operations onto NATS KV primitives. Reconnection and bucket handle refresh are fully automatic: an epoch-based staleness check at the top of every operation transparently re-opens the KV handle after any NATS disconnect/reconnect cycle.
Beyond the standard CacheDB API the module provides:
Atomic CAS counters — cache_add and cache_sub use compare-and-swap with a configurable retry budget (nats_cas_retries default 10), giving safe concurrent counter increments from multiple &osips; worker processes.
JSON full-text search index — keys whose names begin with fts_json_prefix are automatically parsed and indexed in a SHM-backed hash table (sharded under NATS_IDX_SHARDS = 16 gen_lock_set locks). cache_query performs O(1) equality lookups against this index without scanning the KV bucket. The bucket count is tunable via the index_buckets modparam. The index can be disabled entirely via enable_search_index = 0 for PK-only deployments (notably usrloc-as-store), in which case cache_query uses a PK fast path that goes straight to kvStore_Get with no index lookup.
Live KV watcher — a self-healing pthread watches the KV bucket in real time, updating the search index on every put/delete/purge and raising the E_NATS_KV_CHANGE EVI event for script reaction. Skipped entirely when enable_search_index = 0.
nats_cdb_request() — synchronous NATS request/reply (RPC), callable only off the SIP request path (it blocks the worker for the full RTT/timeout).
nats_kv_history() — retrieves the full version history of a KV key as a JSON array.
Raw query commands — KV KEYS, KV PURGE, and KV BUCKET INFO via cache_raw_query.
Map operations — composite key:subkey addressing for get, set, and remove of individual fields within JSON documents.
Dependencies
&osips; Modules
The following modules must be loaded before this module:
None. The module is self-contained and carries its own copy of the shared NATS connection pool (statically linked from lib/nats/libnats_pool.so). It does not depend on the nats_connection module or any other &osips; module.
External Libraries or Applications
The following libraries must be installed before running &osips; with this module loaded:
nats.c with the per-key KV TTL API (libnats.so):
The official C client library for NATS, available at https://github.com/nats-io/nats.c. Build with CMake and install to a standard library path (e.g. /usr/local/lib/libnats.so, then run ldconfig). This module requires a nats.c that provides the per-key KV TTL API (kvStore_CreateStringWithTTL, nats.c PR #1000) — not merely JetStream KV support. The exact release matters less than the capability: an older libnats that predates that API will be detected at build time and the NATS modules will be skipped with a clear message ("libnats too old: missing the per-key KV TTL API ... install a newer nats.c e.g. to /usr/local + ldconfig, or set LOCALBASE") rather than failing with an obscure compile error. pkg-config (libnats.pc) or LOCALBASE is used to locate the headers.
For TLS connections, nats.c must be built against OpenSSL (CMake -DNATS_BUILD_WITH_TLS=ON). The same OpenSSL installation used by &osips; must be present at runtime to avoid library version conflicts.
NATS Server with JetStream enabled:
NATS Server 2.9 or later with JetStream enabled (jetstream: {} in the server configuration). For production deployments a three-node JetStream cluster is recommended to survive single-node failures with kv_replicas set to 3.
Exported Parameters
cachedb_url (string)
The CacheDB URL that associates this module with a named cache group in the &osips; script. The URL format follows the standard &osips; CacheDB convention:
nats[:group]://host:port/
The optional group label (e.g. nats:mycluster://) distinguishes multiple cache namespaces in the same script. When nats_url is also set it takes precedence over the host:port portion of this URL for the actual NATS connection; the URL is then used solely for group registration.
This parameter may be set more than once to register multiple cache group names backed by the same NATS connection.
Set cachedb_url parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats://127.0.0.1:4222/") modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats:calls://127.0.0.1:4222/") ...
Use the nats cache group in a routing script
... cache_store("nats", "call:$ci", "$fu"); cache_fetch("nats:calls", "call:$ci", $var(caller)); ...
nats_url (string)
Comma-separated list of NATS server URLs used as the bootstrap seed list for the connection pool. After the initial connection is established, nats.c discovers all cluster peers via the INFO protocol gossip mechanism and maintains the full topology automatically. This parameter only needs to contain enough servers to bootstrap the initial connection.
Use DNS hostnames rather than IP addresses so that getaddrinfo re-resolves on each reconnect attempt, automatically following topology changes in the cluster.
When this parameter is set it takes precedence over the host:port embedded in cachedb_url.
Default value is nats://127.0.0.1:4222.
Set nats_url parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "nats_url", "nats://nats-1:4222,nats://nats-2:4222,nats://nats-3:4222") ...
kv_bucket (string)
The name of the NATS JetStream KV bucket that this module will use for all key-value operations. If the bucket does not yet exist it will be created during child_init with the replication, history, and TTL settings defined by the kv_replicas, kv_history, and kv_ttl parameters.
If the bucket already exists the creation parameters are ignored and the existing bucket configuration is used as-is. To change replication on an existing bucket use the NATS CLI: nats kv update <bucket> --replicas=N.
Bucket names must be valid NATS subject tokens (alphanumeric, hyphens, and underscores; no spaces or dots).
Default value is opensips.
Set kv_bucket parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_bucket", "opensips-prod") ...
kv_replicas (integer)
The JetStream replication factor for the KV bucket. A value of 3 means every key-value entry is replicated to three NATS nodes, tolerating single-node failure without data loss. This parameter only takes effect when the bucket is first created; it is ignored if the bucket already exists.
For development or single-node deployments use a value of 1.
Default value is 3.
Set kv_replicas parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_replicas", 1) ...
kv_history (integer)
The number of value revisions to retain per key. A higher value allows nats_kv_history to return more historical entries and enables the CAS retry loop in atomic counter operations to succeed under higher write contention. This parameter only takes effect when the bucket is first created.
Default value is 5.
Set kv_history parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_history", 10) ...
kv_ttl (integer)
Bucket-level TTL in seconds. When greater than zero, all keys in the bucket automatically expire after this many seconds since their last modification. A value of zero disables expiry.
Note: this kv_ttl is bucket-wide and only takes effect when the bucket is first created. The expires argument to cache_store and cache_add is accepted for API compatibility but does not set a per-key TTL on these generic paths. Per-key expiry is honoured on the usrloc row-write path, which re-asserts a per-message Nats-TTL on NATS JetStream 2.11+ and is backed by the reaper timer — see the usrloc playbook.
Default value is 0 (no expiry).
Set kv_ttl parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_ttl", 3600) ...
fts_json_prefix (string)
Key name prefix that marks documents for inclusion in the in-process JSON full-text search index. Any key stored via cache_store whose name begins with this prefix is parsed as a JSON object and indexed for equality queries via cache_query.
The index maps every top-level string and integer field of the JSON document to the document key, enabling O(1) hash lookups. Documents stored without this prefix are still accessible via the standard KV operations but are not indexed.
Default value is json_.
Set fts_json_prefix parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "fts_json_prefix", "usrloc.") ...
fts_max_results (integer)
The maximum number of documents returned by a single cache_query call. Limits memory consumption for broad queries that would otherwise match a large fraction of the index.
Default value is 100.
Set fts_max_results parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "fts_max_results", 500) ...
kv_watch (string)
A NATS subject pattern that restricts the KV watcher to a subset of keys. NATS wildcard tokens are supported: > matches any remaining subject tokens (e.g. usrloc.> watches all keys under usrloc.), and * matches exactly one token. When this parameter is not set, the watcher subscribes to all keys in the bucket.
This parameter may be set more than once to watch multiple disjoint key patterns simultaneously. Each call adds one pattern to the watch list; all patterns are passed to kvStore_WatchMulti as a combined subscription. If no kv_watch parameters are set, the watcher is not started.
Default value is NULL (watcher disabled unless at least one kv_watch pattern is configured).
Set kv_watch parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_watch", "usrloc.>") modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_watch", "json_>") ...
TLS configuration
NATS-side TLS for cachedb_nats is configured via OpenSIPS's central tls_mgm module — the same way proto_tls, event_rabbitmq, rest_client, and other TLS-using modules do it. cachedb_nats does not carry its own tls_* modparams.
To use TLS, define a tls_mgm client domain named "nats" and use a tls:// URL in cachedb_url (or set nats_url directly to a tls:// URL):
tls_mgm-driven NATS TLS
loadmodule "tls_mgm.so" modparam("tls_mgm", "client_domain", "nats") modparam("tls_mgm", "certificate", "[nats]/etc/opensips/nats-cert.pem") modparam("tls_mgm", "private_key", "[nats]/etc/opensips/nats-key.pem") modparam("tls_mgm", "ca_list", "[nats]/etc/opensips/nats-ca.pem") modparam("tls_mgm", "verify_cert", "[nats]1")
loadmodule "tls_wolfssl.so" # or tls_openssl.so
loadmodule "cachedb_nats.so" modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats:loc://tls://nats.example.org:4222/")
Plaintext-only deployments (nats:// URLs) don't need tls_mgm loaded — cachedb_nats declares it DEP_SILENT and only attempts the lookup when a tls:// URL is actually used. Operators who configure tls:// without a tls_mgm "nats" domain see a clear error at connect time pointing at the missing config.
tls_mgm's ca_directory field (a directory of PEM files) is supported: cachedb_nats reads every .pem in the directory in lexicographic order, concatenates the contents in memory, and hands the result to libnats via the PEM-string API. This mirrors OpenSSL's SSL_CTX_load_verify_locations(NULL, dir) semantics.
nats_request_max_reply (integer)
Maximum number of bytes accepted in a NATS request/reply response from nats_cdb_request(). Caps the per-call pkg_malloc() in the worker so a malicious or misbehaving responder cannot exhaust the per-worker package memory pool by returning an oversized reply. Replies whose declared length exceeds this bound are rejected with an LM_ERR and nats_cdb_request() returns -1.
Default value is 65536 (64 KB).
Raise the cap for an application that returns large blobs
modparam("cachedb_nats", "nats_request_max_reply", 262144)
nats_request_default_timeout_ms (integer)
Default timeout (milliseconds) used by nats_cdb_request() when the script call passes 0 or a negative value as the timeout argument. Any positive caller-supplied timeout is clamped to the range [10, 30000] ms; values above the upper bound trigger a one-shot LM_WARN. Tightened from "no default" to a fast-fail value so a misconfigured caller cannot block a SIP worker for the full upper-clamp duration.
Default value is 500 (ms).
nats_cas_retries (integer)
Number of compare-and-swap retries used by atomic counter increments (cache_add()) and JSON field updates (cache_update()). Each retry costs one round-trip to the NATS server. When the retry budget is exhausted the operation is dropped and an LM_WARN is emitted naming the affected key.
Raised from a hardcoded value of 3 (which silently dropped increments under as few as 3 concurrent writers) to a default of 10; operators on hot-contested keys can raise further.
Default value is 10. Minimum bound is 1.
index_buckets (integer)
Hash bucket count for the in-memory JSON-FTS search index. Higher values reduce average chain length on the lookup path at the cost of a larger fixed allocation for the bucket array (~32 KB per doubling).
The init code rounds the requested value up to the next power of two with a floor of 16 (NATS_IDX_SHARDS) so the hash function can use a bitmask AND instead of a modulo. Operators setting non-power-of-two values will see the rounded count logged at LM_INFO on startup.
Recommended values: 4096 at <= 20 000 AoRs, 16384 at 100 000, 65536 at 1 000 000. Above 1 000 000 endpoints the recommended path is to disable the index entirely via enable_search_index = 0; see doc/SCALING.md for the analysis.
Ignored when enable_search_index = 0.
Default value is 4096.
Set index_buckets parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "index_buckets", 16384) ...
enable_search_index (integer)
Whether to maintain the in-memory JSON-FTS search index. When set to 0 the module:
Skips nats_json_index_init in mod_init; the global index handle stays NULL.
Skips the initial KV-driven index build and the watcher thread in child_init.
Routes every cache_query through the PK fast path (single-condition is_pk=1 filter only); non-PK and multi-condition filters are rejected with an explicit error message.
Routes every cache_update through its long-existing PK branch; non-PK updates are likewise rejected.
This is the recommended setting for cluster_mode = full-sharing-cachedb usrloc deployments at every scale. usrloc's read (cdb_load_urecord) and write (cdb_flush_urecord) paths construct cdb_filter_t with is_pk = 1, so the index adds only cost. Disabling it saves the SHM footprint (~250 MB at 1MM endpoints, ~2.5 GB at 10MM), avoids watcher-thread CPU saturation at scale, and removes the per-write index update from the CAS hot path.
Leave at 1 only for mixed deployments that also serve script-driven non-PK cache_query calls against the same bucket.
See doc/SCALING.md and the usrloc playbook () for guidance on when to flip this knob.
Default value is 1 (enabled).
Disable the search index for usrloc deployments
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "enable_search_index", 0) ...
dedicated_watcher_proc (integer)
Whether to move the KV watcher out of the rank-1 SIP worker (where it lives as a pthread) and into a dedicated OpenSIPS child process. When set to 1:
The module attaches a proc_export_t entry to its exports, so the OpenSIPS core forks one extra child whose main loop is nats_watcher_proc_main.
The rank-1 child_init skips pthread_create, so the watcher does not also run inside the SIP worker.
The dedicated process maps the same SHM-backed JSON-FTS index that every SIP worker reads (g_idx is allocated pre- fork in mod_init). The per-shard locks added in commit 43ceca02b serialise the cross-process writes safely without any new synchronisation.
destroy skips nats_watch_stop -- the pthread state lives in another process and pthread_join from main would never return. The OpenSIPS core delivers SIGTERM to the dedicated process directly at shutdown.
Only meaningful when enable_search_index = 1. When the index is disabled there is no watcher to relocate, so the dedicated process is neither declared nor forked regardless of this setting (a warning is logged at LM_INFO if both are set).
Worth flipping at ≥ 100k AoRs with the index on, where the steady-state watcher event rate (~1 700 events/s, ~17 % of one core) stretches rank-1's SIP request-handling latency. At smaller scales the rank-1 pthread is essentially free; the dedicated process just adds an extra address space and a duplicate NATS connection. See doc/SCALING.md "Watcher CPU at scale" for the threshold table.
Has no effect when no kv_watch pattern is configured -- there is nothing for the watcher to do, so the dedicated process is not forked (matching the rank-1 pthread gate).
Default value is 0 (legacy rank-1 pthread topology).
Enable the dedicated KV-watcher process
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "enable_search_index", 1) modparam("cachedb_nats", "dedicated_watcher_proc", 1) modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_watch", "json_>") ...
index_resync_on_reconnect (integer)
Whether the KV watcher rebuilds the in-memory search index in full after each reconnect to NATS. The watcher subscribes with UpdatesOnly, so writes made by sibling instances while this process was disconnected are never delivered live. The stale-entry self-heal in nats_cache_query only evicts index entries the KV store reports as gone; it cannot discover a key that was never indexed locally. The default is therefore on, so the index always converges after an outage.
Set to 0 only in large-index or hot-reconnect deployments that cannot afford the O(N) bulk rebuild on every reconnect; bound the staleness window with index_resync_interval_secs (the periodic resync timer) instead, since per-query self-heal does not recover missed inserts.
Ignored when enable_search_index = 0.
Default value is 1.
index_resync_interval_secs (integer)
Periodic full rebuild of the in-memory search index on a timer. Belt-and-braces upper bound on per-process index staleness regardless of reconnect cadence or self-heal pace. Skipped silently while NATS is disconnected.
Set to a value <= the longest tolerable staleness window in deployments where the lazy self-heal pace is insufficient. Each tick costs one kvStore_WatchAll snapshot drain.
Ignored when enable_search_index = 0.
Default value is 0 (disabled).
map_legacy_read (integer)
Map operations store each field under a subject composed as enc(key) '.' enc(field), both components hex-escaped, so the broker lists one map with an exact server-side filter (enc(key).>) instead of scanning every key in the bucket. Entries written before this format change use a : separator that cannot be filtered server-side.
While this is on, map_get and map_remove run an additional whole-bucket scan for the legacy key: prefix so existing maps keep working across the upgrade. That pass is O(total keys); after migrating with the nats_map_migrate MI command, set this to 0 to drop the compat scans and make map operations purely O(matches).
Default value is 1 (legacy reads enabled).
cdb_drain_timeout_ms (integer)
Shutdown drain timeout in milliseconds for the shared lib/nats connection pool. Controls how long nats_pool_finalize waits for in-flight publishes / requests to complete before forcibly closing connections. Cross-DC deployments with high RTT may need to extend the budget; the underlying setting is shared with event_nats (last writer wins).
Default value is 5000 ms.
reconnect_wait (integer)
The time in milliseconds to wait between reconnection attempts when the NATS connection is lost. Applies to the shared connection pool managed by lib/nats.
Default value is 2000 ms.
Set reconnect_wait parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "reconnect_wait", 1000) ...
max_reconnect (integer)
Maximum number of reconnection attempts during child_init. Once the initial connection is established, the nats.c library handles runtime reconnection autonomously with no limit. This parameter affects only the startup retry loop, not runtime resilience.
Default value is 60 attempts.
Set max_reconnect parameter
... modparam("cachedb_nats", "max_reconnect", 30) ...
Exported Functions
nats_request(subject, payload, timeout_ms, result_pvar)
Performs a synchronous NATS request/reply operation. Publishes payload to the NATS subject subject and waits up to timeout_ms milliseconds for a reply. When a reply arrives the payload is stored in result_pvar.
This function implements the standard NATS request/reply pattern: the sender publishes to a subject and includes a unique reply-to inbox. A responding service publishes to that inbox. This is suitable for synchronous RPC calls to external services such as authentication validators, fraud scoring engines, or routing decision services.
This function can be used from any route.
Parameters:
subject (string) — NATS subject to send the request to. May contain &osips; pseudo-variables.
payload (string) — Request payload, typically a JSON string. May contain pseudo-variables.
timeout_ms (integer) — Maximum wait time in milliseconds. If no reply is received within this window the function returns -2. Values <= 0 are replaced by nats_request_default_timeout_ms (default 500); positive values are clamped to [10, 30000] ms.
result_pvar (writable pvar) — Pseudo-variable to receive the reply payload string. Reply size is capped by nats_request_max_reply (default 64 KB); oversized replies are rejected.
When the underlying NATS connection pool is in disconnected state, the function fast-fails with -1 without attempting the publish, rather than blocking the worker for the full timeout_ms.
Return values:
1 — reply received successfully.
-1 — error (NATS pool disconnected, connection failure, publish error, or reply over nats_request_max_reply).
-2 — timeout, no reply received within the effective timeout_ms.
nats_request usage
... $var(req) = "{\"user\":\"" + $fU + "\",\"domain\":\"" + $fd + "\"}"; nats_request("auth.check", $var(req), 2000, $var(reply)); if ($retcode == 1) { xlog("auth service replied: $var(reply)\n"); } else if ($retcode == -2) { xlog("auth service timed out\n"); sl_send_reply(503, "Service Unavailable"); exit; } ...
nats_kv_history(key, result_pvar)
Retrieves the revision history for a KV key and stores it as a JSON array string in result_pvar. Each array element is an object with the fields rev (revision number, integer), value (the stored string at that revision), and ts (Unix timestamp of the revision in nanoseconds).
The depth of history available depends on the kv_history bucket setting. With the default of 5, up to five past values are returned per key in reverse chronological order (most recent first).
This function can be used from any route.
Parameters:
key (string) — KV key to retrieve history for. May contain pseudo-variables.
result_pvar (writable pvar) — Pseudo-variable to receive the JSON history array.
Return values:
1 — history retrieved successfully.
-1 — error (NATS failure or allocation error).
-2 — key not found or no history available.
nats_kv_history usage
... nats_kv_history("usrloc.alice", $var(hist)); if ($retcode == 1) { # $var(hist) = [{"rev":3,"value":"sip:alice@192.0.2.1","ts":1700000003}, # {"rev":2,"value":"sip:alice@192.0.2.2","ts":1700000002}, # {"rev":1,"value":"sip:alice@192.0.2.3","ts":1700000001}] xlog("history for alice: $var(hist)\n"); } ...
nats_kv_get(bucket, key, value_pvar, [revision_pvar])
Reads a key from the JetStream KV bucket and stores its value in value_pvar. The optional revision_pvar receives the current revision number; pass it to a subsequent nats_kv_update call to perform a Compare-And-Swap update.
The bucket must already exist. At mod_init time the module creates the bucket named by the kv_bucket modparam; if you call nats_kv_get with a different bucket name that bucket must have been pre-created out of band (e.g. via the nats CLI or another opensips instance).
CAS contract for revision_pvar: the wrapper populates the script var with both string and integer views so it can drive a subsequent nats_kv_update CAS. NATS revisions are 64-bit; the &osips; script integer type is 32-bit. In the (unrealistic) case that a key passes INT_MAX revisions the wrapper logs a WARN and the truncated value will fail any subsequent CAS.
Return values: 1 success; -1 error (no NATS connection, bucket missing, etc); -2 key not found.
nats_kv_get usage with CAS read
$var(rev) = 0; if (nats_kv_get("calls", "$ci", $var(state), $var(rev))) { xlog("call $ci is in state $var(state) at rev $var(rev)\n"); }
nats_kv_put(bucket, key, value)
Unconditionally writes value at key in bucket. Any concurrent writer's revision is overwritten. Use nats_kv_update instead when you need to preserve optimistic-concurrency semantics.
Return values: 1 success; -1 error.
nats_kv_update(bucket, key, value, expected_rev)
Compare-And-Swap update: writes value at key only if the bucket's current revision for that key equals expected_rev. Read the rev with nats_kv_get; pass it back here.
expected_rev accepts both a literal integer and a script variable (e.g. $var(rev)). When passing a variable the var must have been written by nats_kv_get / nats_kv_revision -- those wrappers mark the var with the int type flag the &osips; fixup engine requires.
Return values: 1 CAS succeeded; -1 error (connection, permissions, malformed publish, etc — not retryable); -2 CAS mismatch — the key's current revision is not expected_rev; a concurrent writer beat us, so re-read the revision and retry. The two are now cleanly separated: -2 is returned only for a genuine revision conflict (the broker's wrong last sequence / jsErrCode 10071), while any other failure is -1, so a CAS retry loop cannot spin on a non-retryable error. In script, retcode is FALSE for both -1 and -2; if you need to distinguish them inspect $retcode directly.
Idempotent state transition with CAS
$var(rev) = 0; if (!nats_kv_get("calls", "$ci", $var(s), $var(rev))) return;
if (!nats_kv_update("calls", "$ci", "ringing", $var(rev))) { xlog("CAS failed: someone else updated $ci, rc=$retcode\n"); # caller can retry, surface 503, etc. }
nats_kv_delete(bucket, key)
Removes key from bucket. Subsequent nats_kv_get on the same key returns -2 (not found). Past revisions remain visible to nats_kv_history until the bucket's history window rolls them off.
Return values: 1 success; -1 error.
nats_kv_revision(bucket, key, revision_pvar)
Convenience wrapper around nats_kv_get that fetches only the current revision number, without copying the value out. Same int-type contract on the returned var as nats_kv_get -- safe to drive nats_kv_update.
Return values: 1 success; -1 error; -2 key not found.
CacheDB Operations
All standard &osips; CacheDB script functions are supported. The engine identifier in every call must match the group name used in cachedb_url (default: nats).
KV Key Validation
Every KV operation (cache_store, cache_fetch, cache_remove, cache_add, cache_sub) passes its key through validate_kv_key() before reaching the NATS server. Invalid keys are rejected with -1 at the module layer (surfacing to scripts as the standard cachedb failure code) rather than being delegated to NATS, which would otherwise fail silently or return opaque protocol errors.
A key is considered invalid if it is empty, or contains any of the following:
control characters (< 0x20 or 0x7f DEL)
whitespace (space or tab)
NATS subject wildcards (*, >)
the : character (reserved by the $KV.<bucket>.<key> subject mapping)
Dots, hyphens, underscores, slashes, equals signs, and backslashes are allowed — dots separate subject tokens in the underlying NATS representation. Use dots or dashes when composing structured keys (e.g. call.abc123, user-alice).
cache_store — KV put
Stores a string value under the given key in the NATS KV bucket. The key must consist of valid NATS subject tokens (alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, and dots as separators; no spaces or colons). If the key already exists it is overwritten.
When the key begins with fts_json_prefix and the value is a valid JSON object, the document is parsed and added to the in-process search index so that it becomes immediately queryable via cache_query.
cache_store with nats
... # Plain key-value cache_store("nats", "call.$ci", "$fu|$tu");
# JSON document (auto-indexed because key starts with "json_") cache_store("nats", "json.user.alice", "{\"name\":\"alice\",\"domain\":\"example.com\",\"status\":\"active\"}"); ...
cache_fetch — KV get
Retrieves the current value of a key from the NATS KV bucket and stores it in the given pseudo-variable. Returns 1 on success, -2 if the key does not exist, and -1 on error.
cache_fetch with nats
... cache_fetch("nats", "call.$ci", $var(call_info)); if ($retcode == -2) { xlog("no active call record for $ci\n"); } ...
cache_remove — KV delete
Removes a key from the NATS KV bucket. The operation is a soft delete that creates a deletion tombstone while preserving revision history. Use the KV PURGE raw query command to permanently remove a key and its full history.
cache_remove with nats
... cache_remove("nats", "call.$ci"); ...
cache_add — atomic counter increment
Atomically increments an integer counter stored in the KV bucket. The operation uses compare-and-swap (CAS) with up to nats_cas_retries retry attempts, safely handling concurrent increments from multiple &osips; worker processes. If the key does not yet exist it is created with an initial value equal to increment.
The counter is stored as a plain decimal integer string in the KV bucket. The expires argument is accepted for API compatibility but does not set a per-key TTL (NATS KV TTL is bucket-wide; see kv_ttl).
cache_add with nats
... cache_add("nats", "counter.calls.$fU", 1, 0); cache_counter_fetch("nats", "counter.calls.$fU", $var(call_count)); xlog("$fU has $var(call_count) active calls\n"); ...
cache_sub — atomic counter decrement
Atomically decrements an integer counter. Equivalent to cache_add with a negated increment value. Uses the same nats_cas_retries budget as cache_add (default 10).
cache_sub with nats
... cache_sub("nats", "counter.calls.$fU", 1, 0); ...
cache_counter_fetch — read counter value
Reads the current integer value of a counter key. Returns 1 on success (counter value in pvar), -2 if the key does not exist, and -1 on error.
cache_counter_fetch with nats
... cache_counter_fetch("nats", "counter.calls.$fU", $var(n)); if ($var(n) > 5) { sl_send_reply(503, "Too Many Calls"); exit; } ...
JSON Full-Text Search Index
The cachedb_nats module maintains a process-local hash-based search index over JSON documents stored in the KV bucket. Documents are included in the index when their key begins with the string defined by fts_json_prefix (default: json_). All top-level string and integer fields of each document are indexed as field:value entries mapping to the document key.
The index supports equality queries via the standard CacheDB cache_query call:
cache_query — JSON equality search
... # Find all documents where the "domain" field equals "example.com" cache_query("nats", "domain", "example.com", $var(results)); if ($retcode == 1) { xlog("matched documents: $var(results)\n"); } ...
Index lifecycle:
Startup: the index is built by scanning the entire KV bucket during child_init. Every key matching fts_json_prefix is fetched and parsed.
Live updates: the KV watcher thread calls nats_json_index_add or nats_json_index_remove for every put, delete, or purge event received from the NATS server.
Reconnection: when the watcher detects that a reconnect epoch change has occurred, it performs a full nats_json_index_rebuild — clearing all existing entries and repopulating from the current KV state. This guarantees the index never drifts after a disconnect.
Thread safety: all index operations acquire a dedicated pthread_mutex_t. The KV watcher thread (writer) and &osips; SIP worker processes (readers via cache_query) contend on this mutex. Index memory is managed with raw malloc and free rather than &osips; pkg_malloc, because the watcher pthread and SIP worker thread share the same process address space and pkg_malloc is not thread-safe across pthreads. Statistics counters use __atomic_fetch_add for lock-free cross-thread accumulation.
The maximum number of results returned per query is controlled by fts_max_results.
KV Watcher
The KV watcher is a self-healing pthread that runs inside the first &osips; SIP worker process (rank 1) and monitors the NATS KV bucket for real-time mutations. It is started by nats_watch_start during child_init when at least one kv_watch pattern is configured.
Only a single watcher per &osips; instance is started (on rank 1) to minimise JetStream ordered consumer count. Each watcher creates one JetStream ordered push consumer in the NATS server; during cluster topology changes nats.c rebalances these consumers internally, and fewer consumers reduce the window for race conditions during failover.
The watcher runs a four-step loop:
Wait for connectivity: polls nats_pool_is_connected until the NATS connection is established. Sleeps briefly between polls to avoid busy-waiting.
Rebuild index: acquires a fresh KV handle from the pool and calls nats_json_index_rebuild to repopulate the search index from the current bucket contents. This is the crash-recovery path that ensures the index is consistent after any disconnect.
Create watcher: calls kvStore_WatchMulti with the configured key patterns to create a kvWatcher handle.
Process updates: calls kvWatcher_Next in a loop. For each received kvEntry the watcher:
Updates the JSON search index (nats_json_index_add for put, nats_json_index_remove for delete or purge).
Raises an E_NATS_KV_CHANGE EVI event carrying the key, operation type, value (on puts), and NATS revision number.
If a reconnect epoch change is detected the loop exits back to the connectivity-wait step.
The _watcher_running flag is an atomic_int accessed via atomic_load and atomic_store. The nats_watch_stop function signals the thread to exit, stops the kvWatcher to unblock kvWatcher_Next, then joins the thread. It is safe to call during &osips; shutdown regardless of whether the watcher was ever started.
Raw Query Commands
The module exposes NATS KV administrative operations via the standard CacheDB cache_raw_query interface. The following commands are supported:
KV KEYS
Lists all keys currently present in the KV bucket. The result is stored in the provided pseudo-variable as a comma-separated string of key names.
KV KEYS raw query
... cache_raw_query("nats", "KV KEYS", $var(keys)); xlog("all KV keys: $var(keys)\n"); ...
KV PURGE <key>
Permanently removes a key and its entire revision history from the KV bucket. Unlike a soft delete (which leaves a deletion tombstone), a purge is irreversible and frees the storage used by all historical revisions.
KV PURGE raw query
... cache_raw_query("nats", "KV PURGE json.user.alice"); ...
KV BUCKET INFO
Returns a JSON object describing the KV bucket configuration: bucket name, replication factor, history depth, TTL, and the backing JetStream stream name.
KV BUCKET INFO raw query
... cache_raw_query("nats", "KV BUCKET INFO", $var(info)); xlog("bucket info: $var(info)\n"); ...
Map Operations
The module supports composite key:subkey addressing for reading and writing individual fields of JSON documents stored in the KV bucket. These operations are accessible via the column-oriented CacheDB API functions (map_get, map_set, map_remove) which are used internally by &osips; modules such as usrloc when configured with a CacheDB backend.
map_get — retrieves the JSON document at key and returns its top-level fields as separate columns in a cdb_res_t result set.
map_set — reads the existing JSON document at key (or starts from an empty object), merges the provided field-value pairs, and writes the result back using CAS for consistency.
map_remove — reads the existing JSON document, removes the named field (subkey), and writes the result back. If no fields remain after removal the entire key is deleted from the bucket.
Exported MI Functions
nats_kv_status
Returns a JSON object with the current KV bucket configuration and NATS connection state. Useful for monitoring and diagnostics.
Response fields:
bucket (string) — name of the configured KV bucket.
replicas (integer) — configured replication factor.
history (integer) — configured history depth per key.
ttl (integer) — configured bucket TTL in seconds (0 = no expiry).
connected (string) — yes if the NATS connection pool is currently connected, no otherwise.
nats_kv_status MI call
## opensipsctl mi nats_kv_status { "bucket": "opensips", "replicas": 3, "history": 5, "ttl": 0, "connected": "yes" }
nats_cdb_stats
Returns a snapshot of the cachedb_nats counter set, summed across the per-process slots maintained in shared memory. Used for alerting and capacity planning; see the usrloc playbook () for recommended thresholds.
Counters in the response:
cas_retry — total CAS retries across all cache_add / cache_sub / cdbf.update calls. Bumped once per failed kvStore_Update.
cas_exhausted — total cdbf calls that ran out of nats_cas_retries attempts and dropped the write. Should be 0 in steady state; any non-zero value means writes were lost.
create_doc — total times nats_cache_update synthesised a seed document and called kvStore_CreateString (the insert path).
index_miss_kv — query/update found a key in the in-memory search index but the KV store said NOT_FOUND. Flags index staleness, typically from a sibling-instance delete. Sustained non-zero values indicate cross-instance churn worth investigating.
nats_cdb_stats MI call
## opensipsctl mi nats_cdb_stats { "cas_retry": 142, "cas_exhausted": 0, "create_doc": 5034, "index_miss_kv": 3 }
nats_map_migrate
Rewrites every legacy : separated map entry into the new . separated, hex-escaped format in place (read the old key's value, write it under the recomposed key, delete the old key). Any key containing : is a legacy map entry, so non-map keys are untouched. The command is idempotent: a migrated key carries no raw :, so re-running migrates only whatever remains in the old format.
Name: nats_map_migrate. No parameters. Returns scanned / migrated / skipped / failed counts. Run it once after upgrading, confirm failed is 0, then set map_legacy_read=0 to drop the compat scans.
nats_map_migrate MI call
## opensips-cli -x mi nats_map_migrate { "scanned": 10432, "migrated": 318, "skipped": 0, "failed": 0 }
Exported Events
E_NATS_KV_CHANGE
Raised by the KV watcher thread for every mutation received from the NATS KV bucket. This event allows &osips; routing scripts to react to changes made by any client in the cluster — not just changes made through &osips; itself.
The event is raised with the following parameters:
key (string) — the KV key that was modified.
operation (string) — the type of mutation: put (value created or updated), delete (soft delete tombstone), or purge (hard delete with history removal).
value (string) — the new value of the key. Only present for put operations; absent for delete and purge.
revision (integer) — the NATS JetStream sequence number (revision) of this mutation. Revisions are monotonically increasing and globally ordered within the KV bucket.
Subscribing to E_NATS_KV_CHANGE in opensips.cfg
... startup_route { subscribe_event("E_NATS_KV_CHANGE", "nats:opensips-events"); }
event_route[E_NATS_KV_CHANGE] { xlog("KV change: key=$param(key) op=$param(operation) rev=$param(revision)\n"); if ($param(operation) == "put") { xlog(" new value: $param(value)\n"); } } ...
Cluster Configuration
For production deployments a three-node NATS JetStream cluster is recommended. The nats_url parameter is a seed list used only for the initial bootstrap connection. After the first connection is established, nats.c discovers all cluster peers via the INFO protocol gossip mechanism (connect_urls) and maintains the full topology automatically. Adding or removing nodes from the cluster is transparent to the &osips; module.
Use DNS hostnames rather than IP addresses in nats_url. The nats.c library calls getaddrinfo on each reconnection attempt, so DNS-based hostnames automatically pick up topology changes such as node replacement or IP address reassignment.
The kv_replicas parameter controls the JetStream replication factor and only takes effect when the KV bucket is first created. A value of 3 on a three-node cluster tolerates one-node failure without data loss or availability impact. To change the replication factor on an existing bucket use the NATS CLI:
nats kv update opensips --replicas=3
The internal reconnection loop is unlimited at runtime: nats.c will never permanently remove a server from its routing pool. The max_reconnect parameter only limits the number of retries during the initial connection phase in child_init.
Three-node cluster configuration
... loadmodule "cachedb_nats.so"
modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats://nats-1:4222/") modparam("cachedb_nats", "nats_url", "nats://nats-1:4222,nats://nats-2:4222,nats://nats-3:4222") modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_bucket", "opensips") modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_replicas", 3) modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_history", 5) modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_ttl", 0) modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_watch", "usrloc.>") modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_watch", "json.>") ...
Single-node development configuration
... loadmodule "cachedb_nats.so"
modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats://127.0.0.1:4222/") modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_replicas", 1) modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_watch", ">") ...
&playbook;
